content.method: Longitudinal cohort study linking secondary-school track placement to administrative labor-market records (earnings, employment stability) using regression discontinuity around admission cutoffs to control for selection; comparison with matched near-threshold non-selected students over a 10-15 year horizon.
content.question: Do short-term achievement gains for students selected into 6/8-year gimnázium programs translate into long-term labor-market earnings or career-quality benefits, or do they primarily reflect test-score inflation and peer-composition effects without persistent human-capital gains?
content.why_it_matters: This bears directly on whether the efficiency case for maintaining tracks (elite-school directors' pedagogical-value claim) offsets the equity costs documented across lenses. It is foundational to weighing T1 (status quo) against reform options, since if gains are illusory, the case for preserving tracks weakens substantially.
